title: Listening - Part 4: Returning to University
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five mature students discussing their reasons for returning to university and their experiences

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d been a successful travel agent for years, but [21]I decided to go back to university to study History because I’d always felt a sense of regret about not finishing my degree when I was younger[/21]. I wanted to prove I could do it. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive boost in my ability to analyze complex information at work[/26]. I’m much better at spotting patterns and potential issues in our travel contracts now. It’s made me much more efficient. || Speaker 2: I was a police officer for twenty years, and [22]I enrolled in a Law degree primarily to prepare myself for a second career in legal consultancy after my retirement[/22]. I wanted a smooth transition. However, [27]the biggest challenge was the initial feeling of being totally out of my depth with the academic writing style[/27]. I was used to writing police reports, not complex legal essays. It took a long time to find my academic voice, but it’s been incredibly rewarding. || Speaker 3: I’m a mother of three, and [23]I went back to study Psychology because I wanted to understand the behavioral issues my youngest son was facing[/23]. I wanted to be a better parent. [28]What I found most difficult was the struggle to balance my studies with my family responsibilities[/28]. I was often writing essays at 2 AM. It was a physical and mental marathon, but the sense of achievement at graduation was worth every sleepless night. || Speaker 4: I’d been a retail manager, and [24]I chose to study Environmental Science because I felt a desperate need to contribute to something more meaningful than just sales targets[/24]. I wanted a career with a purpose. [29]The most rewarding aspect has been the wonderful social circle I’ve found among students of all ages[/29]. I’ve made friends with twenty-year-olds and sixty-year-olds. It’s broadened my horizons and challenged my assumptions about younger generations. || Speaker 5: I recently went through a period of unemployment, and [25]I returned to university because I wanted to update my skills and make myself more competitive in the modern job market[/25]. I wanted a fresh start. [30]The most significant outcome has been the discovery of my own intellectual resilience[/30]. I’d forgotten how much I enjoyed the process of learning. It’s given me a sense of confidence and energy that has completely changed my outlook on the future. I feel younger than I have in years.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to return to university.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 wanted to finish her degree and resolve a past regret.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to prepare for a second career in legal consultancy.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to understand her son's behavioral issues.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted a more meaningful career in environmental science.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to update his skills to be more competitive after unemployment.

options.A: to win a scholarship for further research
options.B: to increase their employability in a new field
options.C: to support a family member's needs
options.D: to fulfill a requirement for their current job
options.E: to find a more ethical professional path
options.G: to facilitate a transition to a different role
options.H: to address a sense of unfinished business

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome or difficulty.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a boost in her ability to analyze information at work.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 found the academic writing style very challenging compared to police reports.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 struggled with the work-life balance and late-night studying.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the social connections she made with students of all ages.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 highlights the discovery of her intellectual resilience and confidence.

options.A: a decision to change their career path entirely
options.B: a newfound sense of mental strength
options.C: the pleasure of winning an academic prize
options.D: a positive transfer of skills to their current job
options.E: the difficulty of paying for the tuition fees
options.F: the effort required to balance multiple duties
options.G: struggling with a specific type of task
options.H: the benefit of a diverse social group
